observatory-work-1.text: The observatory is open to everyone. Anyone can use its capabilities to capture images of celestial objects. Moreover, the website features a section

about.title: About the Project
about.description: Learn more about the "Look at the Stars" project, our team, and our homemade astronomical observatory. Join us and discover the beauty of space!
about.intro: , and this website is my attempt to tell you about my hobby, bring together all the information, and capture the achievements made on the journey of exploring space. Astronomy for me is not just a hobby, but an entire world that changed my life. Thanks to it, I found incredible friends, learned so much, and realized how vast and mysterious the Universe truly is. This project is our joint contribution to popularizing and advancing astronomy. We sincerely believe that through our efforts, space will become closer and more accessible to everyone.
about.telegram-channel: Subscribe to this project's Telegram channel
about.team-description: We work in various fields, but we are all united by one thing — a love for the stars and the night sky. Since 2016, we have been promoting astronomy, organizing star parties in the Orenburg region, and hosting sidewalk astronomy evenings in Orenburg. Together we gaze at the stars, share knowledge, and inspire others.
about.project-title: The "Look at the Stars" Astronomy Project
about.project-description: In 2016, we launched the "Look at the Stars" project in the Orenburg region to make astronomy accessible to everyone. Since then, every season we host free open-air lectures, organize out-of-town trips with telescopes, and hold informal sidewalk astronomy evenings.
about.project-goal: The main goal of the project is to showcase the grandeur and beauty of the Universe and inspire people to explore space. We cover not only the Orenburg region but also neighboring areas, traveling to locations where the night sky is especially bright. Learn more about our current and past events on the page
about.project-sidewalk: , and for those who want to see the stars within the city, we have prepared a section
about.project-sidewalk-link: Where to Look Through a Telescope in Orenburg
about.project-locations: Our events take place not only in cities and towns of the Orenburg region but also in neighboring areas. We organize excursions to remote locations where minimal light pollution allows you to enjoy the starry sky to the fullest. Our team of amateur astronomers and volunteers shares their knowledge and experience so that everyone can discover the beauty and mysteries of the Universe.
about.project-lectures: One of the most important parts of the project is the lectures given by experienced amateur astronomers. We talk about scientific discoveries, the history of astronomy, and its modern achievements. The lectures are held in an accessible format where complex topics are explained in simple and clear language. This helps listeners not only broaden their horizons but also inspires them to study astronomy on their own.
about.project-sidewalk-astronomy: We pay special attention to sidewalk astronomy evenings. These open-air gatherings take place in the most accessible parts of the city. At these events, anyone can walk up, look through a telescope, and learn more about what the starry sky conceals.
about.observatory-intro: This project is one of the most significant milestones of my life. Our homemade astronomical observatory is a unique phenomenon for the Orenburg region. It operates autonomously and enables capturing images of deep-sky objects, making astronomy accessible to everyone. You can learn more about the operation and equipment of the observatory in the section —
about.observatory-work-2: , where you will find a FITS file archive. This data can be used for education, asteroid searches, astrophotography creation, and other scientific or creative purposes.
about.observatory-photos-1: Soon we will launch a free telescope control service so that even more people can experience the beauty of the night sky. Don't forget to check out the section
about.observatory-photos-2: , where images captured by the observatory and at our star parties are collected. These photographs have been processed by me and my friends and reflect our passion for space.
about.observatory-conclusion: This project is our contribution to popularizing astronomy. We sincerely hope it will inspire you to discover the Universe!
about.your-help-intro: The development of the "Look at the Stars" project has been made possible in large part thanks to the support of the "Rodnye Goroda" ("Native Cities") social investment program by Gazprom Neft and the assistance of the Orenburg Media news agency team.
about.your-help-description: Our homemade observatory project has also resonated with many supportive participants. We have big plans for developing this direction, and your support plays a key role. We are grateful to everyone who helps with advice, consultations, and recommendations. If we accidentally omitted anyone from the acknowledgments list, please let us know — it is very important to us.
about.your-help-conclusion: Thanks to your help, space truly becomes closer!

index.title: "Look at the Stars" Project
index.description: Look at the Stars — a unique project in the Orenburg region: telescope observations outside the city, sidewalk astronomy, an observatory in the Orenburg region, and astrophotography

objects.title: Astronomical Objects Catalog
objects.description: A catalog of astronomical objects imaged by the observatory: galaxies, nebulae, asteroids, and comets. The objects table includes information on total exposure, availability of processed photos, and data for each filter: Luminance, Red, Green, Blue, Ha, OIII, SII. Learn more about each object and observation details recorded by the observatory.

observatory.title: Observatory
observatory.description: A homemade amateur astronomical observatory with remote access from anywhere in the world via the internet. Observatory operation statistics, number of captured frames, and accumulated data. Telescope work calendar.
observatory.text: Observing the Universe begins here — in real time you can see current weather conditions, camera feeds, equipment status, and the imaging schedule. The observatory operates in semi-automatic mode, enabling remote deep-sky object imaging. An intelligent control system has been implemented that analyzes weather conditions and automatically decides when to start operations. Learn more about the equipment and operating principles on a dedicated page:

mount.title: Mount: Sky-Watcher EQ6
mount.description: A reliable and precise mount ensuring telescope stability even during long exposures.

telescope.title: Telescope: Sky-Watcher BK2001P
telescope.description: A reflector telescope with a 200 mm aperture, ideal for observing deep-sky objects.

main-camera.title: Main Camera: ASI ZWO 6200MM Pro
main-camera.description: A high-sensitivity astronomical camera with 24 MP resolution, capable of capturing the finest details of distant objects.

guide-camera.title: Guide Camera: QHY QHY5
guide-camera.description: A camera for precise pointing and tracking of objects.

guide-scope.title: Guide Scope: SV106 Guide Scope 50mm (Helical Focuser)
guide-scope.description: A compact telescope for guiding during long-exposure imaging sessions.

focuser.title: Focuser: ZWO EAF
focuser.description: An electronic focuser providing precise focus adjustment.

filter-wheel.title: Filter Wheel: ZWO EFW 8x31mm
filter-wheel.description: A versatile filter wheel for working with various spectral ranges.

filters.title: Filters: ZWO L, R, G, B, Ha, OIII, SII (1.25")
filters.description: A set of filters for obtaining color images and narrowband imaging.

coma-corrector.title: Coma Corrector: Baader 2" Mark III MPCC
coma-corrector.description: Eliminates optical distortions, ensuring image sharpness.

observatory.description-1: The observatory's primary focus is deep-sky astrophotography — imaging objects such as nebulae, galaxies, and star clusters. The equipment and technologies used at the observatory produce high-quality images that can be used for both scientific research and educational purposes.
observatory.description-2: The observatory operates in semi-automatic mode, allowing it to be controlled remotely from anywhere in the world via the Internet. This makes it accessible to amateur astronomers, researchers, and enthusiasts worldwide.
observatory.description-3: The observatory is open to collaboration with amateur astronomers, researchers, and educational institutions. We are happy to provide access to our data for scientific and educational purposes. If you would like to become part of our community or use our resources for your projects, please contact us through the feedback form.
observatory.description-4: The Orenburg Observatory is a unique combination of modern technology and the enthusiasm of amateur astronomers. We strive to make astronomy accessible to everyone interested in the mysteries of the Universe. Join us to explore the endless expanses of space together!
observatory.equipment-description: The instruments and technologies we use ensure high observation accuracy and professional-quality images. Here is the main equipment used in our work:
observatory.photos-and-data: A dedicated section of the website features <photosLink>photographs</photosLink> of captured deep-sky <objectsLink>objects</objectsLink>. For each object, detailed parameters and characteristics are available, such as distance, size, spectral data, and much more. These materials can be useful for both beginner astronomers and professionals.

observatory-weather.title: Orenburg Observatory Weather
observatory-weather.description: Live weather charts from the weather station of an amateur observatory on the outskirts of Orenburg. Temperature, humidity, cloud cover, and wind speed in real time.

photos.title: Astrophoto
photos.description: A collection of astrophotographs of galaxies, star clusters, nebulae, planets, and other celestial objects captured by an amateur observatory. Find and filter images by categories and parameters to see unique images of the Universe with details on each filter and exposure.

photo.description: {{photoTitle}} — a photograph of an astronomical object taken at a homemade observatory, equipment used: {{equipment}}.

stargazing.title: Star Parties
stargazing.where_link: Where to Look Through a Telescope in Orenburg
stargazing.description: Star parties in Orenburg — exciting trips outside the city where telescopes open up cosmic horizons. Evenings of scientific discoveries, lectures, and lively conversation with like-minded enthusiasts. Learn the rules of conduct and prepare for stargazing.
stargazing.telegram-subscription: To stay updated on announcements, subscribe to our Telegram channel
stargazing.text-part-1: A star party in Orenburg is a chance to touch the mysteries of the Universe by observing the starry sky in complete darkness. We invite you on exciting trips outside the city, where telescopes open up cosmic horizons right in the fields of the Orenburg region.
stargazing.text-part-2: Every star party is an evening of scientific discoveries, inspiring lectures, and lively conversation with like-minded people. You will learn how our events work, familiarize yourself with the basic rules of conduct at star parties, and be able to prepare for stargazing in advance.

checkin.title: Participant Check-in
checkin.duplicate-qr: This QR code has already been scanned!

entry.show-qr: Show this QR code at the entrance

stargazing-faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
stargazing-faq.description: Find answers to common questions about star parties: registration, what to bring, cost, duration, and how to get there. Prepare for a comfortable night under the stars!

where.question: ❓How do I find out where the event takes place?
where.answer: Our star parties always take place outside the city, usually within 40–70 km. We drive away from light pollution and city lights to see the stars. The exact location becomes available after registration. If you lose the location, go back to the registration page using the same link. There you'll find the geolocation — Yandex Maps and Google Maps.

registration.question: ❓Can I come without registering?
registration.answer: No, you cannot. Registration is required as it allows us to plan the event, notify participants of any changes, and ensure comfort for everyone.

qr.question: ❓Why do I need a QR code?
qr.answer: The QR code is your entry ticket to our event. It will be checked at the entrance. Please have it ready in advance to avoid congestion at the site. If you don't have a QR code, we will have to deny entry. The QR code is our way of managing the number of participants at the venue. Our natural landscape and the team's physical capabilities have their limits. Please respect this and be understanding.

what-to-bring.question: ❓What should I bring?
what-to-bring.answer: For a comfortable stay at the observation field, be sure to bring warm clothing (we're in the steppe — it's windy and cold at night), a camping mat, folding chairs, and blankets. We also recommend thermoses with tea/coffee and sandwiches. Bug repellent is advisable. Hookahs, barbecues, alcohol, and cigarettes are prohibited! Also, we have the cleanest venue around. After every star party, we don't find a single wrapper or piece of trash — and we really appreciate that!

no-car.question: ❓I don't have a car, what should I do?
no-car.answer: We don't organize transportation or provide rides. Our job is to put on an amazing event, and yours is to get there. But for your convenience, we've created a chat
no-car.answer2: . Join, meet people, and find a ride. Everyone there is friendly, communicative, and reliable. Want to chat about space topics? Join the themed

public-transport.question: ❓Can I reach the star party location by public transportation?
public-transport.answer: Unfortunately, no. The venue is located far from public transportation routes. You can get there by personal car or join other participants if they are willing to give you a ride. We recommend arranging this in advance through our Telegram channel.

start-time.question: ❓What time does it start?
start-time.answer: Each star party announcement includes a specific start time. This time it's 9:30 PM. Guest arrival at the venue is from 8:00 PM to 9:00 PM. This means we ask you to arrive early — listen to music, take photos with the Moon and in the sunflower fields, watch the sunset, and breathe in the steppe air. And importantly, park your car to avoid congestion. We really appreciate it!

price.question: ❓How much does it cost?
price.answer: All our star parties are free. The project is growing and developing, and we always welcome support from our audience and subscribers. For that, there are
price.answer2: . If you'd like to support us, we'd be truly grateful!

duration.question: ❓How long does a star party last?
duration.answer: On average, the official program — from the start of the lecture to telescope observations — lasts about 2 hours. But we guarantee they'll fly by. After that, it's up to you — stay and continue stargazing with the telescopes, or head home. Approximate end time: 12:30 AM.

weather.question: ❓What if it's cloudy or raining?
weather.answer: If the weather forecast is unfavorable (clouds or precipitation), the event may be cancelled or rescheduled. We always notify participants about such changes through our Telegram channel, so be sure to follow the updates.

telescopes.question: ❓Do I need to bring my own telescope?
telescopes.answer: No, bringing a telescope is not required. We always have several telescopes available for public use. If you have your own instrument, feel free to bring it — our experts will be happy to help you with setup and usage.

kids.question: ❓Are children welcome?
kids.answer: Absolutely! We're always delighted when the younger generation takes an interest in science! However, the recommended age is 6 and older. Trust our experience on this.

photo.question: ❓Can I take photos?
photo.answer: Of course! You can take photographs, but please follow etiquette so as not to disturb other participants. Do not use bright flashes or flashlights, as this can ruin the night observation.

merch.question: ❓How and where can I buy your merchandise?
merch.answer: We bring hoodies, t-shirts, car stickers, postcards, and sticker packs to the observation field. Want to support the project? Get our merch!

cancel.question: ❓I registered but can't make it. What should I do?
cancel.answer: Simply cancel your booking using the same link. Free spots will appear on the website, and other people will be able to register.

more-people.question: ❓I registered 1/2/3/4/5 people — can I bring more?
more-people.answer: No, you cannot. Our rules are the same for everyone. Only the number of people you registered will be admitted to the venue with your QR code. No exceptions.

late-registration.question: ❓Registration is closed and I missed it. Can I still come? Please!
late-registration.answer: No, unfortunately. Our rules are the same for everyone. We introduced the registration system for a reason and monitor entry to the observation field.

support.question: ❓I love what you do! How can I support you?
support.answer2: . We are constantly purchasing equipment, upgrading our technical capabilities, creating merchandise, and organizing activities. Your support means ❤︎❤︎❤︎

feedback.question: ❓Where can I leave a review about you?
feedback.answer: Our group is called "Look at the Stars" across all social media platforms. We value feedback and always read your reviews. Thank you for them!

stargazing-howto.title: How Star Parties Work
stargazing-howto.description: Learn how star parties work in Orenburg: registration, space lectures, telescope observations, and learning to navigate the night sky. Subscribe to the "Look at the Stars" Telegram channel and join unique astronomical events under the starry sky!
stargazing-howto.unique_event: Every star party is unique, but we follow a general plan to make the event interesting and convenient for all participants.
stargazing-howto.event_announcement_text: Star parties in Orenburg are held in favorable weather conditions, so the exact date is usually known only 2–3 days before the event. We announce upcoming events in our Telegram channel "Look at the Stars", where we publish announcements and important updates. Subscribing to the channel is essential so you don't miss a new star party.
stargazing-howto.registration_text: Participation requires advance registration through our website. It's free and takes just one minute. Please note: beware of scammers — if anyone asks for payment to participate, it's a fraud! We welcome donations, but participation is always free.
stargazing-howto.location_text: Star parties are held outside the city, away from urban light pollution, usually about 40 km from Orenburg. The exact location will be revealed after registration. You need to get to the observation field on your own, so plan your route in advance. The last stretch of the journey is a dirt road about 300 meters long.
stargazing-howto.arrival_text: Arrive 15–20 minutes early to find a comfortable spot on the observation field. Bring folding chairs or mats for comfortable seating.
stargazing-howto.lecture_text: As dusk falls, our astronomy lecture begins. We show videos and photos on a large screen, talking about space in an accessible and engaging way. Lecture topics vary — from "The Perseid Meteor Shower" to "What Black Holes Conceal". The lectures are designed for participants aged 8 and up and last about one hour.
stargazing-howto.orientation_text: After the lecture, we teach you how to find the main stars and constellations by pointing them out in the sky. This part lasts about 20 minutes.
stargazing-howto.telescope_observation_text: Several telescopes are set up at the observation field, each pointed at different celestial objects. You can look through the telescopes, ask questions, and chat with the astronomers.
stargazing-howto.event_duration_text: The entire star party takes 2 to 4 hours. You can leave at any time, but many stay until the very end, enjoying the views of the starry sky.
stargazing-howto.recommendations_text: Please read the "Star Party Rules of Conduct" section to ensure the event is comfortable and safe for everyone.
stargazing-howto.thematic_evenings_text: Sometimes we hold sidewalk astronomy events right in the city. Read more about such events in the "Where to Look Through a Telescope in Orenburg" section.
stargazing-howto.faq: Didn't find the answer to your question? Check out the section
stargazing-howto.telegram: or write in our Telegram channel

stargazing-rules.title: Star Party Rules of Conduct
stargazing-rules.description: Learn the rules of conduct at star parties in Orenburg: what not to do while observing stars and planets through a telescope. Follow these guidelines to make the star party safe and comfortable for all participants.
stargazing-rules.welcome: Welcome to star parties in Orenburg, organized by the "Look at the Stars" project! To ensure that observing the starry sky and planets together is comfortable and safe for all participants, it is important to follow a few simple rules. These guidelines help preserve the atmosphere of nighttime observation and guarantee that everyone can enjoy viewing stars and planets through a telescope.
stargazing-rules.prohibited-title: What Is Prohibited at Star Parties

prohibited-items-1.title: Using bright light sources
prohibited-items-1.description: Flashlights, phone screens, and any bright lighting devices disrupt dark adaptation of the eyes, reducing visibility of the starry sky. Use only red flashlights or special light filters for your devices.

prohibited-items-2.title: Building campfires or using open flames
prohibited-items-2.description: Star parties are held in fields and open areas where there is a high risk of fire. Building campfires is strictly prohibited.

prohibited-items-3.title: Consuming alcohol or smoking
prohibited-items-3.description: Alcoholic beverages, cigarettes, e-cigarettes, vapes, and hookahs are prohibited. This is not only a matter of fire safety but also a sign of respect for other participants who value clean night skies and fresh air.

prohibited-items-4.title: Making noise and disturbing other participants
prohibited-items-4.description: Loud music, shouting, or other sharp sounds can distract and interfere with concentration during telescope observation. Please respect the quiet and the mood of those around you.

recommendations-items-1.title: Protect the environment
recommendations-items-1.description: Take all your trash with you after the observations. We strive to leave star party locations as clean as they were before our arrival.

recommendations-items-2.title: Follow safety precautions
recommendations-items-2.description: Move carefully to avoid damaging equipment and to prevent injury.

recommendations-items-3.title: Respect other participants
recommendations-items-3.description: Everyone came to the star party to enjoy the beauty of space. Treating each other with respect makes the atmosphere friendly and comfortable.

stargazing-rules.importance-title: Why Is It Important to Follow the Rules?
stargazing-rules.importance-description: Following these guidelines helps make stargazing in Orenburg as comfortable as possible for everyone. We create an atmosphere where every participant can witness amazing celestial phenomena, study planets through a telescope, and have an unforgettable experience.
stargazing-rules.join-description: Want to learn more about our events organized in Orenburg?

stargazing-where.title: Where to Look Through a Telescope in Orenburg
stargazing-where.description: Find out where you can look through a telescope in Orenburg. Sidewalk astronomy: free observations of the Moon, planets, and stars on city streets. Want to see more? Head out of the city to observe nebulae and galaxies!
stargazing-where.intro: Sidewalk astronomy is a unique format that brings space closer to everyone. In spring, summer, and autumn, we set up telescopes right on the sidewalks and squares of Orenburg so that anyone can walk up and look at the stars, planets, and the Moon for free. It's a great opportunity for city residents to get acquainted with astronomy in a relaxed and friendly atmosphere.
stargazing-where.announcement-1: We almost always publish announcements for sidewalk astronomy evenings on our
stargazing-where.announcement-2: , but sometimes you can find our telescopes on city streets without any advance notice. One of the project's inspirers, astronomy lecturer Vladimir Ivanovich, often shares his knowledge by showing interesting astronomical objects to anyone interested. Just look for a telescope — and you're sure to get an unforgettable tour of the starry sky!
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-title: What Can You See Through a Telescope from the City?
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-description: Under urban light pollution conditions, the brightest objects remain visible. For example, you can observe:
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-1: Craters and mountains on the Moon, especially impressive during the first and last quarter phases;
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-2: The rings of Saturn, which never fail to amaze;
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-3: Bright stars and double stars, such as Albireo;
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-4: Planetary alignments, if they occur at the right time of year.
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-conclusion: However, city lighting limits our capabilities. Nebulae, galaxies, meteor showers, and even large star clusters often remain hidden from view.
stargazing-where.want-to-see-more: Want to See More? Head Outside the City!
stargazing-where.want-to-see-description-1: To observe faint objects such as the Orion Nebula, the Andromeda Galaxy, meteor showers, and star clusters, you need to travel away from city lights. In places like the Orenburg steppe or other remote areas, the night sky is stunningly beautiful.
stargazing-where.want-to-see-description-2: We regularly organize astronomical field events where you can enjoy the full range of celestial objects. Follow announcements on our "Star Parties" page or in the Telegram channel to join one of these observations.

star-map.title: Star Map
star-map.description: A star map featuring galaxies, nebulae, comets, supernovae, and other celestial objects captured by an amateur telescope
